title: Grips: the only part of the club you actually touch
microFact: Worn, shiny grips force you to squeeze harder — and grip tension is the silent killer of golf swings.
lesson:
GRIPS wear out: sweat, sun, and Bangalore humidity harden and glaze them. A shiny, slick grip makes you clench, and a clenched grip locks your wrists and forearms → lost speed and slices. Regrip roughly once a year for a weekly player (₹300–700 per grip fitted in India).

SIZE matters: grips come undersize, standard, midsize, jumbo. Quick check: grip a club with your left hand — your fingertips should LIGHTLY touch your palm. Digging in = too small (encourages hooks); not reaching = too big (encourages slices, blocks). Fitters size off your hand measurement in seconds.

While we're at your hands: the GLOVE goes on your LEFT hand (right-handed player), exists for grip not warmth, and comes off for putting — watch any good player peel the glove walking onto the green and hang it half-out of a pocket. Replace it when it goes shiny. A crusty, board-stiff glove is another quiet tell.

Pressure rule you'll hear forever: hold the club like a tube of toothpaste with the cap off — firm enough to control, soft enough not to squeeze.

quiz.question: Which hand wears the glove for a right-handed golfer?
quiz.explanation: The lead (left) hand — it's the connection point that bears the grip pressure. Most players remove it to putt.
